data.title: Kemira: Long-Term Growth Potential Remains Intact
data.description:
Introduction

Kemira (OTCPK:KOYJF) has released its financial results for 2024, and those results were pretty flat as there were no surprises (nor positive nor negative). I originally liked the company for its relatively defensive characteristics, as this specialty chemicals company is very active in the water treatment industry. The exposure to water solutions and water treatment is the main reason why I’m interested in this Finnish company.

The company sold its oil & gas division last year and this immediately improved the balance sheet as the net debt ratio dropped to less than 0.3 times EBITDA.

Kemira has its main listing on the Helsinki Stock Exchange, where it's trading with KEMIRA as ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Finland is approximately 180,000 shares per day, making it the most liquid venue to trade in the company’s shares. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article. The current share count is approximately 154 million shares, which results in a market capitalization of approximately 3.34B EUR.

The Cash Flow Is Stronger Than The Net Income

In the final quarter of the year, the company reported a total revenue of approximately 724M EUR, which is a decrease of approximately 10% on a reported basis but keep in mind the Q4 2023 revenue still included the contribution from the oil & gas division. Excluding that division, the underlying revenue actually increased by approximately 5%.

This resulted in an EBITDA of approximately 116.5M EUR and an EBIT of 67M EUR. The net finance expenses were pretty low and this enabled Kemira to report a pre-tax income of almost 62M EUR which yielded 51.1M EUR in net profit. Approximately 3M EUR of the net income was attributable to non-controlling interests, which means Kemira’s own net profit came in at 48M EUR, which represents an EPS of 0.31 EUR.

Looking at the full-year result, Kemira reported a total revenue of 2.95B EUR, which is approximately 10% lower than the revenue reported in 2023. The EBITDA increased by approximately 2% despite having sold the oil & gas division. Looking at the bottom-line result, the 249M EUR in net attributable profit represented an EPS of 1.62 EUR per share.

Looking at the full-year cash flow result, Kemira reported an operating cash flow of just under 485M EUR, but this included a cash tax payment of 89.6M EUR, although only 74M EUR in taxes was owed, as per the income statement. The reported operating cash flow also includes a 28.5M EUR contribution from working capital changes and excludes the almost 32M EUR in lease payments. This means the underlying operating cash flow was approximately 440M EUR on an underlying and normalized basis.

The total capex incurred in 2024 was just 167M EUR, which results in an adjusted free cash flow of 273M EUR. This represents an FCFPS of approximately 1.77 EUR per share.

That being said, as the image below shows, only 106M EUR of the entire capex was classified as maintenance capex with an additional 42M EUR in improvement investments and 20M EUR in growth.

Even if I’d only exclude the growth investments from the equation, the underlying free cash flow result per share would have been around 1.90 EUR. And if we would assume a portion of the improvement capex should be seen as growth capex rather than pure maintenance capex, the net free cash flow per share goes up to around 2 EUR per share.

At the end of 2024, Kemira had approximately 519M EUR in cash on the balance sheet, while the net debt was just 291M EUR (and about half of that net debt consisted of lease liabilities). This means the debt ratio was just around 0.2-0.3 times the lease-adjusted EBITDA. Meanwhile, at the current share price, Kemira is trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 6.75 which is very reasonable.

The Expectations For 2025

The company also already provided an outlook for 2025, and Kemira expects to generate a revenue of 2.8-3.2B EUR and the midpoint of this guidance range represents a 1.5% increase compared to the FY 2024 revenue.

Kemira also published an operative EBITDA guidance of 540-640M EUR and the midpoint of 590M EUR represents a 1% increase compared to the 2024 result. In a way, the 2025 guidance is perhaps a bit disappointing, but fortunately the company will continue to generate plenty of cash flow which it can reinvest in itself.

Kemira also announced a dividend of 0.74 EUR per share based on the FY 2024 results and considering the payout ratio remains low, I think it’s fair to assume the dividend will increase in FY 2025 as well, even if the results remain flat. Unfortunately, Finnish dividends are subject to a 35% dividend withholding tax (investors should consult with a tax specialist to see if and how (a portion of) the Finnish tax can be recovered).

Investment Thesis

While the midpoint of this year’s guidance is ‘uninspiring’, Kemira may perhaps surprise to the upside as its revenue and EBITDA ranges are pretty wide. In any case, the company’s net debt should continue to decrease, and I would expect Kemira to be net debt free by the end of 2026.

I sold my position in Kemira in 2024, but I may have sold too soon, as Kemira’s results are holding up pretty well. I would consider going long again, but I am in no rush.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 4 Macro Scenarios Holding Me Back From TLT (For Now)
data.description:
The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT) was launched by iShares in 2002. As a low-cost, highly liquid fund of US Treasury Bonds, I believe its original intent was to attract investors who wanted to limit volatility in their portfolios.

The problem for anyone who used TLT as part of a defensive 60 / 40 portfolio is that they did not account for the new territories the FED’s monetary policy would enter between 2010 and 2021. As Zero-Interest-Rates-Policy (ZIRP) became the “new norm,” yields on all forms of US debt naturally started to decline over time. Initially, TLT appreciated as an effect of ZIRP - moving from a price in the 90s before 2008 to the 120s from 2012 onwards.

However, after the FED’s hawkish pivot in 2022 brought an end to ZIRP, equities and bonds both saw a significant correction. This was not an issue for investors holding actual US Treasury Bonds (as they could simply wait expiration to recover their capital), but it was (and still is) for those who chose TLT.

TLT, as a passive fund with a rolling portfolio of bonds, does not reimburse its investors of their “principals.” Investors who bought TLT during the ZIRP era saw a significant correction and now have to hope for a return of zero interest rates to recover their capital.

In this regard, TLT has become an odd product. I would not recommend its use to anyone looking for broad exposure to US Treasuries. However, it is perfect to gamble on the direction of interest rates. Even the web page of TLT just vaguely states its objective as “Use to customize your exposure to Treasuries.”

In this article, I present four different macro scenarios concerning the future direction of US interest rates, and particularly the expected US long term “equilibrium interest rate,” or R*, which will ultimately determine TLT’s performance.

TLT is a bet on Macroeconomics: What is R*?

“R*” is the macroeconomic symbol indicating equilibrium interest rate. The equilibrium interest rate is the theoretical real interest rate where the economy operates at full employment and stable inflation, neither stimulating nor slowing growth.

Because TLT is so heavily influenced by interest rates, its performance (and investment thesis) ultimately depends on what is the long term equilibrium interest rate for the US.

The reason why I am focusing on equilibrium interest rates rather than actual interest rates set by the FED is two-fold. First, because provided that an investor has a long enough time horizon, R* is what actually matters in the context of betting on TLT; they can expect interest rates to revert to the equilibrium, eventually.

Second, because I find it makes more sense to reason long-term rather than short-term when it comes to macroeconomics. I believe that long-term macro trends are easier to forecast. In the short term, the vast majority of macro prediction, including those about interest rates, fail (something I will cover in my risk section).

R* in the US has been 0% to 1% for the vast majority of the last 15 years. These were the times of Zero-Interest-Rate-Policy following the 2008 crisis. Historically however, the R* for the US economy was always in the 2% to 4% range before 2008. This was also the case in the 1970s, despite the FED having raised rates in the double digits at times to bring inflation down.

The real question when it comes to TLT, then, is: what is the R* for the US economy, today? Is ZIRP the “new norm” to revert back to, or is a 2% to 4% range to be expected again?

In the next paragraphs, I present two bullish and two bearish short- and mid-term scenarios with four different outcomes in terms of R*.

TLT Bullish scenario #1: inflation under control

My first bullish scenario would play out as follows:

  • US Inflation shows a decisive, declining pace in the upcoming CPI readings

  • As a result, the FED signals 1 to 3 interest rate cuts in 2025 and early 2026

  • Inflation goes back permanently to a sub-2% level, and the long term R* turns out to be between 2% to 3% (reversion to historical norm).

  • Disinflationary policies from the current US admin that may play a role in this scenario are significant cuts in government spending (e.g., as effect of DOGE) or a sudden decrease in oil prices (e.g., as effect of the war in Ukraine coming to an end).

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to have a positive correlation with equity markets. I believe both TLT and generally equities would experience a bullish run, should inflation go back under control relatively quickly and interest rates decline relatively fast.

As a result of this expected positive correlation, I am not particularly interested in investing in TLT at current prices based on this scenario. I am already heavily invested in equities and risk assets such as Bitcoin (BTC-USD), and I am not interested in a relatively modest upside from TLT when I can expect more from other parts of my portfolio.

My expectation for this scenario would be for TLT to reach levels last seen in September 2024 - when inflation was seemingly under control and the uncertainty surrounding a Trump’s election victory wasn’t priced in. My target price here is ~$100 and represents a ~12% upside from TLT’s prices at the time of writing.

TLT Bullish scenario #2: rising inflation triggers a deep recession

My second bullish scenario for TLT would play out as follows:

  • US Inflation starts rising in the next CPI readings (consistent 3%+ figures), indicating it may be re-accelerating well above the FED’s 2% yearly target.

  • As a result, the FED indicates no rate cuts at all for 2025, and/or hints at possible rate hikes in 2025 and 2026, depending on exact inflation trends.

  • Due to the FED actions and overall economic climate, the US enters a deep recession in late 2025 or early 2026.

  • As a result of the recession, inflation craters well below the FED’s 2% target and the FED immediately pivots to sub-2% rates to stimulate the economy. The FED will eventually revert back to ZIRP, meaning R* would fall below 1%.

  • Inflationary policies from the current admin that may play a role in this scenario are significant US trade tariffs (e.g., on the EU, Canada or Mexico) and mass deportations (tightening the job market) igniting inflation in the short-term.

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to decline significantly in the short-term to then significantly bounce once the FED pivots to sub-2% interest rates. It would be the absolute most bullish scenario for TLT if the FED indicated a permanent return to ZIRP, as before the pandemic.

My target price for TLT in this scenario would be in the $ 130 - $ 170 range, assuming a reversion to R* below 1%. These were the levels last seen in 2020, well before post pandemic inflation manifested itself and when the markets were expecting ZIRP to be the new norm for the foreseeable future.

In this scenario, I would most likely get to buy TLT at a discount against current prices - as initial inflation readings and FED pivots to higher rates would punish the fund. This is why I am also not interested in entering a trade on TLT at current prices, based on this bullish scenario. However, should it manifest in the upcoming months, I might revise my recommendation to a BUY (more on this in my conclusions).

TLT Bearish scenario #1: stagflation and Volkerism 2.0

The most worrying scenario for TLT would be that of stagflation. This could manifest as follows:

  • US Inflation starts rising in the next CPI readings (consistent 3%+ figures), indicating it may be re-accelerating well above the FED’s 2% yearly target.

  • As a result, the FED indicates no rate cuts at all for 2025, and/or hints at possible rate hikes in 2025 and 2026, depending on exact inflation trends.

  • Despite the FED’s actions, a US recession does not manifest, or it does but without bringing inflation down.

  • The FED is now stuck between accepting higher inflation as the new norm, or significantly raising rates to hurt the economy (something reminiscent of Volkerism). In either case, I would expect rates to be higher for longer, and R* to stabilize slightly above the historical norm, in the 3% to 4% range.

  • Politics may play a role here should the FED work against the government - for example rising rates while the government hands out money in various forms to appease citizens (e.g., the rumor that Americans will receive handouts from the savings generated by DOGE).

In this scenario, I would once again expect TLT to have a positive correlation with stocks. Both would experience a prolonged bear market, in my view, should a stagflation scenario become reality. This is once again the reason why I am not interested in entering a TLT trade at this moment in time. I would be only if I expected TLT to de-risk my equity-heavy portfolio, which isn’t the case.

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to correct to eventually revert to the same levels of today - pricing in a long term R* of 3% to 4%. Needless to say, this scenario does not represent a compelling investment proposition in TLT at current levels.

TLT Bearish scenario #2: Black Swan event significantly raises R*

My last bearish scenario for TLT is also the least likely to happen, as it concerns a possible black swan event affecting key aspects of the US economy. Ultimately, the equilibrium interest rate depends on many macroeconomic factors, such as the productivity, demographics and savings behavior of actors in an economy.

Should any of these factors be heavily influenced, I would expect R* to move in unexpected ways - potentially stabilizing well above the historical norm, at 5% or more. I will provide two examples of what may trigger such an event.

First, is Artificial Intelligence. AI may very well produce an unexpected, major increase in labor productivity. Should this happen, the US economy may “overheat” to a point that the equilibrium interest rate needed to keep inflation at 2% could become 5% or higher. This scenario would manifest

The second scenario is that of a deadlock between the government and the FED, where the FED is eventually strong handed into accepting 3% as the new inflation target. Should this happen, I would expect R* to stabilize at the very least in a range from 4% to 5%.

A significant raise in R* for the US economy will be very bearish for TLT, and would most likely result in the fund correcting permanently to lower price levels than today.

Conclusion: my strategy to play TLT

I do not believe a compelling thesis exists today to enter a trade in TLT:

  1. If inflation comes back quickly under control, I would expect equities to perform as well if not better than this fund from current prices.

  2. If there is a deep recession, TLT may overperform equities in the mid- and long-term assuming ZIRP comes back, but it will first correct from current levels, representing a better entry point.

  3. If the US enters a stagflationary period and eventually reverts back to the historical norm of 2% to 3% equilibrium interest rates, TLT is already fairly priced.

  4. A black swan event permanently raising US equilibrium interest rates to 5%+ (for example, a sudden increase in labor productivity because of AI) is the most bearish scenario for TLT.

My strategy on TLT for now is to observe. Should the US enter a recession, I will revise my recommendation and - if inflation data signals stagflation is NOT likely - I may enter TLT at a discount against current prices. If I were to enter TLT, I would do so via Long Call options (probably LEAPS), to maximize my upside.

Should inflation come back under control in the next months, I will be OK having “missed out” on TLT, as I expect a relatively limited upside. I would benefit from this scenario in any case, being heavily exposed to other risk assets such as equities.

Risks

Overall, the main element of risk with entering any trade in TLT is that it is historically very hard to predict the direction of interest rates. As the chart above shows, the market is consistently wrong about interest rates, and it tends to underestimate or overestimate any movement in either direction. That's why I have focused my article on R* rather than actual interest rates.

The market is currently expecting one or no interest rate cut in 2025, and there are discussions of possible stagflation. If I had to place a bet based on how wrong the market has historically been, I would say neither scenario is likely. I would rather bet on a slow disinflationary trend, with inflation eventually coming back under control by the end of year. Today, I would also still bet that ZIRP will eventually make a comeback. My belief is rooted in global ageing demographics and the overall impoverishment of the Western middle class - a secular, deflationary trend that I don’t see reversing in the future.

Another risk worth noting with my thesis concerns the specifics of my second bearish scenario. A black swan labor productivity event may end up playing in favor of TLT, contrary to my assumptions. For example, mass layoffs due to AI replacing human input in the job market may more than compensate for increases in labor productivity due to AI. In the context of consumer inflation, wage growth counts far more than productivity and this may be disrupted as a result of AI adoption. However, for the sake of this exercise, I have only assumed a possible negative impact on R* for my scenario.

Finally, there is also a risk in focusing on R*, instead of actual interest rates. Ultimately, R* is a theoretical concept that is never officially declared by the FED, but only inferred by economists. Regardless of what the R* of the US economy is, actual rates may stay significantly above or below it for long periods of time. An example is the 1970s, where rates stayed well above the 2 to 3% theoretical R* for more than a decade.

data.title: Compass Therapeutics: Good Execution, Major Catalysts Ahead
data.description:
I covered Compass Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CMPX), a Boston company with positive data from a South Korean trial, back in August 2023. The data is from South Korea because lead candidate CTX-009, a dual DLL4 x VEGF-A inhibitor, was developed by ABL Bio of South Korea. This molecule, also called tovecimig, targets 2nd line biliary tract cancer. It is running a phase 3 trial, with topline data expected in Q1 2025.

Tovecimig also completed a phase 1/2 monotherapy trial in 3L/4L colorectal cancer back in 2023. One more trial in 2L CRC without chemo will begin this year. Besides tovecimig, there is CTX-471, which completed a basket study in post-checkpoint inhibitor solid tumors, and will begin another phase 2 basket study in NCAM(CD56)-positive cancers. Another molecule, CTX-8371, completed dose escalation and will produce phase 1 data in the second half of 2025. Two other molecules are in preclinical stages.

In that 2023 article, I discussed the Korean data. I will not go into those details now - you can read them here - but I will only note my impression. The data was all over the place. There were a number of nuances in the data:

  • Monotherapy safety profile was good, with no DLTs and the only grade 3 AE being hypertension (2021 data)
  • Combo therapy with paclitaxel had a poorer safety profile, with one death through pneumonia and multiple TEAEs causing discontinuations
  • The above safety data did not compare well with other BTC chemo trials
  • In the monotherapy basket trial, CRC was the most successful indication, however, in general, ORR was not good, while DCR was strong, meaning the drug worked better at the lower end of the disease burden spectrum
  • This was also seen with prior VEGF patients, which improved results in the lower end, with PD and SD patients
  • DLL4+ patients did very well, considering the DLL4 inhibiting nature of the molecule. Gastric patients with DLL4 did the best
  • However, that conclusion is skewed by the fact that there were too few DLL4- patients to compare with

So this is the data - I hope I have been able to give you the impression that the data is scattered and all over the place - because that is the impression I get. The molecule works, but where it works, in what situation and under what circumstances - these things need to be brought out of this morass of data. I hope that will happen with the currently running trial.

This is the COMPANION-02 trial which has a primary completion date of July 2025. This is an US trial, an open-label, randomized, phase 2/3 trial combining chemotherapy paclitaxel versus paclitaxel alone in patients “with unresectable advanced, metastatic or recurrent biliary tract cancers who have received one prior systemic chemotherapy regimen.” Total estimated enrollment is 150 patients - enrollment was completed in August 2024. The primary endpoint is overall response rate (ORR') and secondary endpoints include progression free survival (PFS), overall survival (OS), clinical benefit rate (CBR), and duration of response (DOR).

This trial, I hope, will bring out the right sort of data in the cancer they selected, BTC. Now, the annual incidence rate of BTC in the US is about 8000. 40% of these are intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. BTC is a relatively rare cancer, accounting for just 1% of all cancers. The 5-year survival rate of metastatic BTC is just 5%. The market size is small, projected to be just about $1.4bn by 2030.

A number of treatment paradigms are employed. Because the cancer is often detected in metastatic stages, chemotherapy is standardly used. Targeted and immunotherapy drugs include Pemazyre (pemigatinib) from Incyte Corporation (INCY), Imfinzi (durvalumab) from AstraZeneca (AZN), and the very recently approved Ziihera (zanidatamab) from Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ). To be noted, ORR rate was 52% from the registrational trial that led to approval for the Jazz drug. This is an accelerated approval, and there was a 53% rate of AEs in the trial. Those are the benchmarks for tovecimig.

Among pipeline drugs are a number of molecules from Asian biopharmas like Eisai, Taiho, BeiGene and so on.

Financials

CMPX has a market cap of $423mn and a cash balance of $135mn as of the last reported quarter. R&D expenses were $8.6mn and G&A expenses were $3.6mn. At that rate, they have a cash runway of 10 quarters.

Looking at this from an FCF perspective, their CFO was ($11.1) and CapEx was zero, therefore their FCF was ($11.1). Dividing the cash balance with this, we get a cash runway of 11 quarters.

The company filed a mixed shelf offering for $300mn, however they have not actually diluted yet.

Risks

BTC is a difficult cancer with poor success rates. Moreover, there are a few approved therapies, including the latest from Jazz. Although zanidatamab targets HER2+ BTC while tovecimig targets DLL4+ BTC, one should keep in mind that this is a relatively small market. Other than they, they are sufficiently funded, and there is a major upcoming catalyst.

Also note, CMPX has a pretty low trading volume of just around 31,000, although average volume is good at 1.38mn.

Bottomline

The stock more than doubled after this news announcement on Jan 7 about plans to initiate a trial of CTX-471 in patients with tumors expressing neural cell adhesion molecule, or NCAM/CD56. I haven’t touched on this because it sounded inconsequential at the moment. However, the market clearly took notice. What the company said about the prior data for this molecule is this:

Presented CTX-471 clinical data at multiple scientific meetings in 2024, including data demonstrating durable clinical responses in Phase 1 presented at the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) Annual Meeting and data showing a correlation between levels of NCAM (CD56) expression and disease control presented at the 39th Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer 2024 Annual Meeting.

So anyway, the stock is up a lot on this announcement, and we have a major trial data drop in a few months. I would want the price to settle down a bit before moving in with an investment. But overall, CMPX looks like they are doing good medicine.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cisco Faces Growing Competition For The Hyperscalers
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO) reported a strong turnaround to revenue growth in fiscal Q2 ’25 as the decline in networking sales softened. Management noted in the Q2 ’25 earnings call that customer orders grew by 40% in 1h25, potentially suggesting that customers have worked down their inventory levels and are returning to purchasing new networking equipment.

With the modestly optimistic outlook for e2h25, I’m forecasting Cisco to close eFY25 with net revenue of $59.4b and an adjusted EPS of $3.70/share, a 6% improvement to the top-line and a slight compression at the bottom. Despite these factors, I believe Cisco should realize improved growth in networking equipment sales in the coming years as enterprises turn from AI training to bringing AI applications into the production environment.

At the valuation level, CSCO shares have been experiencing significant expansion to its trading premium, potentially suggesting that investors are valuing CSCO for its AI growth capabilities. With these factors in mind, I am reiterating my BUY rating on CSCO shares with a price target of $70.63/share at 12.75x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Cisco Systems Operations

Cisco realized a strong Q2’25 with new orders growing 29%. AI infrastructure orders by the webscalers grew to $350mm in q2’25 and have cumulated to $700mm YTD, bringing Cisco on track to achieving $1b in eFY25.

Data center switching drove growth as the 800G Nexus switches became more widely adopted. Accordingly, enterprises began ordering AI products like Nexus switches and Unified Computing Systems (UCS) as AI applications move to the production environment.

This is one major area where Arista Networks (ANET) competes with Cisco. Though Cisco’s FY24 networking segment is roughly 5x the size of Arista’s products revenue, Arista is realizing significant growth in the data center space, especially with server interconnects and data center switches. Where Cisco may have experienced a high level of inventory digestion and purchasing fatigue, Arista realized significant growth.

Enterprise spending may continue to grow throughout the duration of eFY25 as more enterprises move from proof-of-concept to production applications with GenAI and AI agents. This shift may drive more campus networking and more spend on data center networking equipment.

Enterprise growth drove new networking product orders for switches, routers, webscale infrastructure, and IoT. Campus switching orders also grew by double digits as Cisco rolls out WiFi 7 access points, primarily driven by the return-to-office phenomenon.

One big paradigm shift worth considering is the transition from AI training to inferencing. I suspect the majority of AI training is being done with servers rented through the cloud service providers ((CSPs)), allowing for compute capacity to be scaled to suit the needs of the training models. I anticipate that much of this will transition to in-house data centers as enterprises transition to bringing their AI applications into production. Given the complexity of AI infrastructure, I believe enterprises will purchase rack-scale solutions that have Cisco’s Hyperfabric and AI pids integrated into the rack. This can potentially be a major growth driver for Cisco in e2h25 and eFY26.

Cisco is also reinventing how businesses can leverage AI applications. In q2’25, Cisco released its new data center Smart Switch and Agile Services Networking. The Smart Switch includes Cisco Silicon One E100 networking chips, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) Pensando DPUs with Cisco Hypershield embedded in the switch. Accordingly, the N9300 Smart Switch will begin shipments in spring 2025 with higher-performance models being first available in 2025.

Industrial IoT also drove significant growth as customers prepare for the deployment of AI-powered robotics and industrial security. Though Cisco does not break out the financial impact of industrial IoT, this sector could be a major growth driver for the company as more manufacturing is brought back to the US and automated. One of Cisco’s competitors in the space is Globalstar (GSAT), a firm that specializes in 5G satellite connectivity for factory automation.

I believe another major area of growth for Cisco will be AI security. One of the biggest concerns in working with external AI models is data leakage, a major threat to private data. For example, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) was found to have a major data leakage, exposing millions of sensitive records on January 29, 2025. According to Wiz Research, the data leak was the result of a misconfigured cloud storage instance. Though the vulnerability was likely brought forth as a result of the heightened scrutiny of DeepSeek given its geographical origins, I suspect DeepSeek is not alone in posing risks as it relates to data security.

Cisco has made a number of AI-oriented acquisitions in the last year with a primary lean towards security. This includes Robust Intelligence and DeepFactor, both in August 2024, and SnapAttack in January 2025. Cisco also acquired Deeper Insights AI in October 2024 to bring in custom-designed AI solutions for enterprises. Management noted in the q2’25 earnings call that they will be focusing on strategically targeting AI companies to strengthen its position in AI. This can potentially lead to Cisco becoming a competing secured-network AI building platform, similar to Cloudflare (NET).

Cisco Systems Financial Position

Cisco grew its total ARR by 22% to $30.1b in q2’25 with subscription revenue now accounting for 56% of total revenue. This transition is working to Cisco’s benefit as it may shed more light into revenue going forward.

Looking to Q3 ’25, I’m forecasting Cisco to generate $13.9b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.91/share. For eFY25, I’m forecasting revenue to remain strong as customers turn from inventory digestion to purchasing, driving total revenue to grow by 6%. I’m forecasting eFY25 adjusted EPS to come in at $3.70/share as a result of slight compression at the bottom line resulting from the debt issued in Q3 ’24.

Cisco ended Q2 ’25 with $16.85b in cash & equivalents and a total debt position of $31b for a net debt/aEBITDA of 0.66x.

Risks Related To Cisco Systems

Bull Case

Cisco is expanding its offerings with embedded AI and security to enhance its capabilities for enterprises and CSPs. Cisco may be moving into the next networking refresh cycle, potentially driving campus and data center networking sales growth. The firm is also expanding its AI footprint through M&A, potentially creating a secure network environment for AI builders, similar to Cloudflare’s offerings.

Bear Case

Cisco may experience competitive pressure from Arista Networks as the firm deepens its partnerships with the CSPs, potentially leading to slower growth in its networking segment. Cisco may also face some margin compression from tariffs placed on infrastructure, potentially leading to some margin compression in the coming quarters.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Management authorized a $15b share repurchase program in q2’25. The share repurchase authorization is in addition to Cisco’s remaining $2b, making the total authorization sum to $17b. Cisco also increased the quarterly dividend rate by $0.01/share to $0.41/share.

On a company comparison basis, CSCO shares trade at a slight discount to its peers in the IT infrastructure and networking industries. Looking to CSCO’s performance throughout 2024-to-date, CSCO’s trading premium has significantly expanded from a low of 10.20x to 18.68x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis. This may mean that CSCO shares are either very expensive on a relative basis or the market is pricing CSCO shares higher to meet the more AI-oriented infrastructure companies.

Using an internal valuation model based on my EBITDA forecast and CSCO shares’ historical trading premiums, I believe shares should be priced using my blue-sky scenario at $70.63/share at 12.75x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA. My rationale behind this is that CSCO shares are more consistently trading at a higher premium, suggesting that the historical premiums may not be suitable for valuing the firm. Given these factors, I am reiterating my BUY rating for CSCO shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative marketcap and relative multiple.

data.title: JD: Earnings To Reflect Q4 Stimulus
data.description:
JD.com (NASDAQ:JD) is due to report Q4 earnings on March 6th. This article looks at why record revenues are expected, and the outlook for the stock going forward.

Context

JD, like many Chinese stocks, bottomed in 2024 and broke out of its downtrend in September 2024 following the announcement of fiscal and monetary stimulus packages.

While this was a convincing break-out, it wasn't driven by anything specific to JD such as earnings. Broader Chinese indices like the Hang Seng also broke out and JD's e-commerce peers Alibaba (BABA) and PDD Holdings (PDD) experienced very similar moves.

One thing to note in the chart above is that both JD and PDD remain below their October high. BABA, on the other hand, has doubled in 2025 and is far above its October high. Last week, the company released impressive earnings with an EPS beat of nearly 10%. Since the stimulus measures were first announced in September, these earnings were the first proper test of their success. It bodes well for JD on March 6th.

Earnings

JD has been posting solid profits despite the Chinese economic slowdown.

The company generated around $4.7B in free cash flow in the first nine months of 2024. This was mostly used for stock buybacks:

The Company repurchased a total of approximately 255.3 million Class A ordinary shares (equivalent of 127.6 million ADSs) for a total of approximately US$3.6 billion during the nine months ended September 30, 2024.

It also means the dividend of $0.76 should be safe.

Here are the expected numbers for Q4.

These are optimistic estimates. There were 12 revisions higher and revenue of $45.8B would be a record.

This isn't necessarily overly optimistic as the general trend for earnings has been higher. Furthermore, BABA just reported record sales.

However, there could be a high bar set for a positive surprise, and good news will be partially priced in.

Valuation

JD has a PE ratio of 13.48, which is in line with PDD. It was also similar to BABA's valuation until BABA surged higher this year.

BABA and JD are different companies, but the chart above illustrates there is scope for multiple expansion in the e-commerce sector. JD's price-to-sales is very low and it's fair to say the company is undervalued.

That begs the question, why?

The Macro

As JD's annual report underlines, "The majority of our net revenues are derived from retail sales in China." Its success hinges on the Chinese consumer.

Most investors will already be aware of the problems facing the Chinese economy. Deleveraging and the popping of the real estate bubble may have been necessary for long-term prosperity, but it created multiple near-term challenges and left a large part of the population in negative equity. Consumer sentiment is in the doldrums.

To make matters worse, the US has imposed tariffs on Chinese exports and could increase them further if another trade war unfolds. JD's valuation is low because of these headwinds and risks.

The Chinese government and the PBOC ramped up stimulus in September with rate cuts and tools to support stock markets. Further pro-growth measures were introduced throughout Q4 and could boost JD's earnings on March 6th.

It may be beneficial that most of JD's sales are in mainland China and it does not rely on exports to the US. The trade war should have little direct impact other than weighing on the economy and if Beijing keeps ramping up stimulus to counteract the tariffs, demand should be healthy. In other words, JD could reap the positives without being directly impacted by the negatives.

Risks

Optimistic earnings estimates could lead to a miss and a negative reaction.

The longer-term risk comes from a continued slowdown in the Chinese economy and the failure of stimulus. An escalation in the trade war with the US is a major macro risk.

The annual report outlines several risks to doing business in China.

Conclusions

JD will report earnings on March 6th and record revenues are expected due to continued growth and the stimulus measures announced last September. This will be the first real test of these measures.

Optimism seems merited, and BABA's earnings and large rally this year have shown there is room to grow in this segment. JD has an attractive valuation and is well-positioned for gains should some of the gloom lift from the Chinese economy in 2025.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: What Mr. Buffett Might Be Thinking
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) is a holding company that owns 189 subsidiaries and invests their money in stocks, treasury, and other securities. Their leader is the famous Warren Buffett, and Berkshire has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) since 1965. The compounded annual gain of Berkshire between 1965 and 2024 is 19.9% versus 10.4% of the S&P 500, and the overall gain during that period of Berkshire is 5,502,284% vs. that of the S&P 500 at 39,054%.

Berkshire just reported their full year 2024 performance, and the report is full of great information and insights. Simply so much to learn from as an investor.

In this article, I will share my analysis and breakdown of Berkshire's annual report and Mr. Buffett's business decisions.

Operational Performance in 2024

When Wall Street talks about Berkshire, a lot of their focus is on what stocks they own: What stock did Warren Buffett buy? Why did Warren Buffett sell this?

However, Berkshire's core businesses are insurance, railroad, energy generation and distribution, and other controlled businesses (e.g., Precision Castparts, Duracell, Pilot Travel Centers, Acme, and etc.) Berkshire's equity holdings (Apple, American Express, Coca-Cola, and etc.) of $272 B is only a portion of Berkshire's total assets of $1.14 T.

Therefore, it is most important to understand how the Berkshire's core business is doing, rather than just what stocks Berkshire is buying or selling.

Here is the segment of the Annual Report that remarks on the matter:

Overall, their businesses performed well in 2024. The total revenue increased from $364 B in 2023 to $371 B in 2024, and the operating earnings increased from $37 B in 2023 to $47 B in 2024. There was substantial performance increase from insurance-underwriting and insurance-investment income.

Here is the summary of how Berkshire did in 2024:

Insurance

Berkshire's core business is insurance, and the major subsidiaries under this segment are GEICO, Berkshire Hathaway Primary Group, and Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance Group.

The insurance segment did very well in 2024. Total revenue from the insurance premium earned increased from $83 B in 2023 to $88 B in 2024. The operating income from insurance-underwriting increased from $5.4 B in 2023 to $9.0 B in 2024, and the operating income from insurance-investment income also increased from $9.6 B in 2023 to $13.7 B in 2024.

The increase of underwriting earnings in 2024 was credited to higher average premiums per auto policy of GEICO, higher property/casualty earnings of Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance Group, and smaller retroactive reinsurance losses of Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance Group.

For 1Q 2025, management estimated the loss from the California wildfire will be about $1.3 B, which, I thought, would be useful info for the future earnings' estimate.

Railroad

Berkshire's Burlington Northern Santa Fe (BNSF) is one of the largest railroad systems in North America, and it serves the Midwest, Pacific Northwest, Western, Southwestern, and Southeastern regions.

Given that they transport products and commodities from manufacturing, agricultural, and natural resource industries, the performance of BNSF is closely related to the general economic conditions of the U.S. and the world.

The railroad operating revenue in 2024 declined by 0.5% compared to 2023, and it was due to lower average revenue per car/unit, lower fuel surcharge, and business mix changes. There was a substantial decrease in coal volume, and it was caused by lower natural gas price over 2024.

Net earnings were $5.0 B for the year, which was 1.1% lower than 2023.

Energy

Berkshire Hathaway Energy Company has many subsidiaries that operate utility companies, natural gas pipelines, liquefied natural gas storage, and other energy interests.

Net earnings attributable to Berkshire shareholders increased substantially from $2.3 B in 2023 to $3.7 B in 2024. The increased earnings were multifaceted. The U.S. utilities, natural gas pipeline, and other businesses all delivered higher net earnings compared to 2023.

Manufacturing, Service, and Retailing

Berkshire has a wide range of manufacturing businesses (industrial, building, and consumer products), and they also have service and retailing businesses like Dairy Queen, Pilot Travel Centers, and Berkshire Hathaway Automotive.

The manufacturing segment had a strong year with both revenue and earnings increases. Total revenue increased from $75 B in 2023 to $77 B in 2024, and the Pre-tax earnings increased from $11.4 B in 2023 to $11.9 B in 2024. The strong performance came from PCC (higher demand for aerospace products and power generation products), Lubrizol (higher demand for specialty chemicals like additives and advanced materials), and Clayton Homes (strong home sales and financial service).

Service and retailing experienced some challenges during 2024. The poor performance of TTI (aviation services) and XTRA (trailer rental & leasing) led to the service groups' pre-tax earnings decline of 23% in 2024 compared to 2023. The retailing business also struggled with a 1.2% revenue decline and 19.2% pre-tax earnings decline.

Investment in Fixed Maturity Securities and Equity Securities

There was a substantial change in Berkshire's fixed maturity security portfolio. Taking advantage of the high-interest rate of the short-term U.S. treasury in 2024, Berkshire invested heavily in short-term U.S. Treasury Bills, and their total holding of short-term U.S. Treasury bills increased from $130 B in 2023 to $286 B in 2024. However, they substantially decreased their investment in equity securities ($353 B in 2023 to $271 B in 2024).

Overall, Berkshire substantially decreased their position in consumer product companies and increased their positions in bank, insurance, and finance companies. The major consumer product holding change came from the sales of Apple (AAPL) stock over the year. Another substantial individual holding change was the reduction of Bank of America (BAC) holdings. However, they still remain a significant portion of Berkshire's portfolio. Apple and Bank of America are 28% and 11% of Berkshire's portfolio, respectively.

Based on these moves, here are a couple of my reads.

Firstly, Mr. Buffett thinks Apple's dominance is wearing off, and he is not expecting that the new initiatives of Apple will be as wildly successful as the iPhone or the MacBook were in the past decade. Also, the tech sector is grossly overpriced at this point. The P/E ratio of NASDAQ is at 48.54x.

Therefore, the expectation of a long term future return is not high for the tech sector, and Apple won't be free from the overall tech sector underperformance.

Secondly, Mr. Buffett expects the overall market performance to be poor for the next couple of years. As of 4Q 2024, the total equity holdings of Berkshire Hathaway is worth $271 B, whereas the total cash and U.S. Treasury Bills is worth $334 B. It's not really hard to guess where Mr. Buffett thinks the better return will come from. The short-term U.S. Treasuries (1 month to 6 months) are yielding about 4.3% per year at this point, suggesting that Mr. Buffett expects the equity return for the next couple of years will be around that point or lower.

Lastly, Mr. Buffett expects poor return from the major banks. He reduced significantly the shares of Bank of America and Citigroup, and his message cannot be clearer than that. The underperformance of major banks could come from expected rate cuts (lower interest rate means lower interest income), economic uncertainties (tariffs, high inflation, and geopolitical tension translates into a high chance of economic problems), and the commercial real estate loan crisis (struggling office building is not news anymore).

Berkshire Hathaway Performance Prediction

Well, it is very hard to bet against Mr. Buffett, who has been outperforming the S&P 500 over several decades. I think Berkshire Hathaway is yet again well positioned to outperform the market. Each of their core businesses (insurance, railroad, manufacturing, energy, and retails) has a strong economic moat around it (Mr. Buffett made sure of that), and the mother company (Berkshire Hathaway) has many resources to support itself if they struggle.

If an economic slowdown comes in 2025, the railroad and retail segment will underperform, but it won't be significant enough to affect Berkshire Hathaway as a whole. Berkshire's equity exposure and U.S. treasury exposure mixture won't deliver a super return, but it will deliver a solid return for the short term.

Risk

Berkshire Hathaway's underperformance will happen if equity markets (S&P 500, NASDAQ, and etc.) go up like they have been in the past couple of years. With the Federal Reserve rate cuts and accommodative corporate tax rate, the stock markets may go up again 20% in 2025, causing the cash and U.S. Treasury heavy portfolio of Berkshire Hathaway to underperform.

The core business of Berkshire is the insurance business. Therefore, large natural disasters can cause substantial losses to Berkshire's core. Actually, Mr. Buffett talks about this risk in his letter to the shareholders. He said "Someday, any day, a truly staggering insurance loss will occur - and there is no guarantee that there will be only one per annum." I tend to agree with his statement.

Conclusion

It's always enjoyable to read the annual report of Berkshire Hathaway and Mr. Buffett's letter to shareholders. They are always full of information and many insights. Also, it's great intellectual fun to analyze his business decisions and to (try to) deduce his intentions from them.

I believe Berkshire Hathaway is well positioned to outperform the stock market yet again in 2025 and beyond, and I'm looking forward to their next report.

data.title: Manulife: 8% Total Yield And Asia Growth A Winning Formula
data.description:
Insurers have long been valued for their stability and reliable dividends. But some, like Manulife Financial (NYSE:MFC), have expanded well beyond traditional underwriting by developing wealth and asset management divisions that create additional sources of growth.

While many peers of similar size focus primarily on North America, Manulife (based in Canada) has established a significant presence in Asia, where it now derives more than half of its earnings. The company’s latest quarterly report reaffirmed this trend, with the Asia segment showing record growth.

The region also presents meaningful risks. Economic uncertainty, regulatory uncertainty, fluctuating exchange rates, and geopolitical tensions all pose potential threats to earnings. With Manulife’s stock trading just below multi-year highs and its price-to-book well above historical levels, potential investors need to weigh whether exposure to Asia is a long-term advantage and/or if the market has already priced in its best days.

This article unpacks Manulife’s business model and presents arguments for and against investment. I touch on comparisons with key peers, Aflac (AFL), Prudential Financial (PRU), and MetLife (MET), particularly regarding valuation and growth catalysts. I conclude that despite a number of risks, some unique to Manulife and others impacting the industry as a whole, the company remains a Buy even at elevated multiples.

A Hybrid Business Model

See Seeking Alpha’s crowdsourced Virtual Analyst Report for a high-level summary of the business and Quant ratings. I’ll briefly contextualize the bull and bear cases below:

Unlike Aflac, which mainly offers supplemental health and life insurance policies, and MetLife, whose asset management business is largely geared toward institutional clients, Manulife has evolved into a hybrid model where wealth and asset management (serving both institutional and retail customers) complements its traditional insurance operations.

Fee-based revenue + insurance premiums = diversified income.

Expansion in Asia has been a big part of Manulife’s strategy. Beyond its strong presence in China, Hong Kong, Japan, and Singapore, the company has been gaining a foothold in parts of Southeast Asia, particularly in Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines. The Asia segment now accounts for 52% of total core earnings, up from 35% in 2019.

Demographics are a key driver of this growth. China’s 60-and-above population is expected to rise to 28% by 2040, and that should fuel more demand for retirement and insurance products. Meanwhile, Southeast Asia is statistically underinsured: Vietnam’s penetration is just ~2.5% of GDP, and Indonesia’s is even lower at ~1.4%. Manulife should be able to capitalize by leveraging its agency networks in these markets.

The Bullish Case

Manulife's Q4 (2024) earnings report provided color on the increasing contribution of its Asia segment, where core earnings rose 27% YoY. Annual Premium Equivalent sales increased 63% in Asia, driving total APE growth of 42% across all segments. New Business Value was also up ~37% reflecting strong demand. For context, China’s middle class is expected to grow by an additional 80 million people by 2030, and the economies of Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines are also growing at 5–6% annually, outpacing most developed markets.

In Hong Kong, Manulife now leads the Mandatory Provident Fund sector with a 28.1% market share and ranks as HK’s top agency for new business growth (see the Q4 slides). While competitors such as Sun Life Financial (SLF) and AIA Group (OTCPK:AAGIY) (OTCPK:AAIGF) also have substantial footprints in Asia, Manulife’s steady expansion in individual insurance and retirement products has been an unequivocal success.

Beyond insurance, the Global Wealth and Asset Management (WAM) division is setting records. Net inflows reached CAD 1.2B in Q4, with total AUM surpassing CAD 1T for the first time. The expansion of Manulife’s capital-light, fee-based WAM operations has boosted return on equity—up to 16.6% in Q4 and approaching management’s long-term target of 18%. This is a significant improvement from an ROE of 12.4% in 2022 and 10.9% back in 2020.

Bolstering the case for investment is the company’s investor-friendly capital allocation strategy. In Q4, Manulife announced a 10% dividend increase and authorized a new share buyback program covering up to 3% of outstanding shares.

Here’s a snapshot of total yield for the trailing twelve months:

Dividend Yield

3.67%

Growth Streak

11 years

5-Year CAGR

9.2%

Payout Ratio

39.9%

Buyback Yield

4.35%

TOTAL YIELD

8.02%

Combining this 8% total yield with the projected ~7% EPS CAGR through 2027 suggests the potential for 15%+ annual gains for investors over the mid-term. Management reaffirmed its commitment to $22B in cumulative cash remittances by 2027, which provides ample support for sustained dividend growth and share repurchases.

I’ll add here that Manulife executed three major reinsurance transactions in 2024, which freed up $2.8B in capital that was previously tied to legacy insurance liabilities. These deals (a large-scale Canadian universal life reinsurance transaction and two LTC transactions) helped reduce exposure to long-term care volatility and allowed Manulife to reallocate resources toward higher-growth, capital-light operations.

The Bearish Case

As I noted in the intro, Manulife’s exposure to Asian markets comes with potential volatility. China’s regulatory landscape has historically been challenging for foreign insurers. (In 2018, for instance, Beijing imposed capital outflow restrictions that temporarily halted cross-border insurance sales.) While the CCP has recently eased some foreign ownership restrictions, allowing firms like Manulife to establish wholly owned subsidiaries, the regulatory framework remains complex and could change overnight. Hong Kong’s ongoing integration with mainland China also muddies the waters in that key market. And to state the obvious, any significant escalation of geopolitical tension between China and Western economies could have far-reaching consequences for multinational firms.

Beyond these macro risks, investment spread compression is a challenge for the industry. As interest rates adjust, insurers face lower yields on new fixed-income investments, which can pressure profitability in traditional insurance segments. Manulife’s U.S. business did report lower spreads in Q4, which weighed on earnings and echoed a broader industry trend.

Currency fluctuations are another risk. Manulife generates a significant portion of its earnings in Asian currencies, particularly the Hong Kong dollar and Chinese yuan. While companies can employ hedging strategies, exchange rate movements still contribute to earnings volatility.

Recently, this has played in Manulife’s favor. Over the past two years, the CAD has depreciated 5.8% against the HKD and 5.3% against the CNY, boosting results by making revenue from these regions more valuable when converted to CAD. Here's the data:

Year

CAD to HKD

CAD to CNY

2023

5.75

5.28

2025

5.43

5.00

Change

-5.8%

-5.3%

This likely played a role in Manulife’s record Asia earnings in recent quarters. But just as favorable movements can boost results, adverse swings in exchange rates could dampen earnings in the future.

On Valuation

From a valuation standpoint, Manulife is trading at nearly 1.7x forward book value, well above its five-year average of 1.1x. While 1.7x still appears attractive relative to peers like MetLife (2.1x) and Aflac (2.2x), the premium might suggest that much of Manulife’s expected growth is already priced in. Manulife’s forward P/E (non-GAAP) of 11.1x is also above that of nearly all its closest peers with the exception of Aflac, the most expensive of the group at 15.5x.

MFC

AFL

PRU

MET

Forward P/E

11.1x

15.5x

8.6x

9.3x

5Y AVG

8.9x

10.5x

8.2x

11.0x

Forward P/B

1.7x

2.2x

1.3x

2.1x

5Y AVG

1.1x

1.6x

1.0x

1.3x

By these numbers, none of the four companies look particularly cheap, though Prudential may superficially appear “least overvalued” and currently has the highest Quant factor grade of the group at B+. But while Manulife’s 1.7x forward book value appears elevated, its rising ROE and earnings growth could support the premium.

Investor Takeaways

Manulife’s Asia segment continues to drive growth, with record APE sales and strong new business value reinforcing its leadership position in several markets. The company’s Global Wealth & Asset Management division has surpassed $1T in AUM, contributing to higher ROE and improving profitability. Its capital return strategy remains highly attractive, with an 8%+ total shareholder yield and $22B in planned cash remittances by 2027. While China’s regulatory landscape, currency fluctuations (recently a tailwind), and investment spread pressures all pose risks, Manulife’s diversified business model and expanding fee-based revenue streams should support resilient earnings over time.

Despite elevated valuation metrics and a 26% increase in share price over the past year, Manulife remains a compelling total return opportunity. I rate the stock a Buy for long-term investors.

data.title: Orogen Royalties: A Solid Company To Participate In The Ongoing Gold Bull Market
data.description:
Orogen Royalties is a prospect generating company that has had significant success over their 15 year history (They were formerly known as Evrim Resources and merged with Renaissance Gold to form Orogen). At this time they have two significant royalties, the first a 2% royalty on the Ermitaño Mine being produced by First Majestic Silver and the second on the Silicon-Merlin deposit being developed by Anglogold Ashanti. The Silicon-Merlin deposit is the best discovery in North America in the last 10 years and as of the latest report hosts more than 16 million ounces of gold and is still growing. Orogen holds a 1% royalty on production from this project.

Orogen Royalties also has dozens of other royalties with partners on less developed projects any of which could develop into significant value added for shareholders. These royalties and partnerships that Orogen creates are fully funded by their partner companies, allowing Orogen to continue to grow their exposure to the gold mining industry without diluting their shareholders. The astute geologists at Orogen have proven to be amongst the most capable exploration teams in the mining industry as the Silicon-Merlin discovery and their recent partnership with BHP (Jan 6. 2025) demonstrates. The partnerships with some of the largest and most successful mining companies in the world demonstrates the quality of Orogen's management and their exploration ideas.

The cashflow from their Ermitaño royalty has allowed the company to continue to grow, stake new projects and acquire non-producing royalties all without the need to issue new shares. They have a strong and committed shareholder base, ~$20million in capital to fund operations and various partners drilling on properties in which they own royalties, with over 100$million spend by project operators in 2023 and this number is likely to continue to grow as they continue to expand their project portfolio and partnerships. It is worth noting that First Majestic Silver continues to expand their Ermitaño mine with new discoveries and developments announced as recently as February 4th 2025. These developments continue to add value to Orogen's royalty portfolio at no cost to Orogen and their shareholders.

When the Silicon-Merlin deposit finally goes into production it will significantly increase the cashflow and profits for Orogen, but in the meantime the stock has steadily increased in price as Anglogold Ashanti continues to drill and expand the size of the deposit (and Orogen's claim to future production). The combination of higher gold prices, growing royalty income, increased spending on development projects and new partnerships makes Orogen Royalties a premier company to own for exposure to the gold sector. Paddy Nicol, CEO at Orogen Royalties, has continued to demonstrate astute business acumen and patient discipline as he has grow the company from a prospect generator with no income into the ultra-successful royalty company it is today. I've been a shareholder for over 10 years and I look forward to the success that Paddy will build upon over the next 10 years. He isn't an old man like many people in the industry and I believe he will continue to succeed in steadily growing Orogen into a household name over the coming decade. It's not too late to jump on board and become a shareholder and in an industry where risk is high and failure is common, Paddy and Orogen have continued provide a low risk investment opportunity that provides the upside potential that many investors looking to participate in the gold industry would be well served to own.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Is A Grave Concern
data.description:
I have been an ardent bull over the past two-plus years on the basis that the economy would eventually land softly, as disinflation restored stable prices, and the Fed reduced its benchmark rate to neutral. As a result, I viewed each pullback and correction along the way as a buying opportunity for long term investors. Last week was the first time I felt like that outlook may be in peril, but I am not yet ready to walk away from the bullish narrative. I am on high alert.

The consensus of consumers is not happy with serious concerns about the outlook for the economy if the Trump administration follows through with its proposed agenda. The University of Michigan’s sentiment survey dropped 10% in February, led by buying conditions for durable goods, which plunged 19%, due to fears that tariffs will increase prices. Inflation expectations in the year ahead jumped from 3.3% to 4.3%, and expectations for the long-term rose to the highest level since 1995. Typically, I do not pay a lot of attention to sentiment surveys anymore, because they have become so partisan that they are useless. Yet with 40% of the electorate now classified as Independents, which outnumbers Democrats and Republicans, I think this report holds some relevance. The Independents now drive the consensus. That importance is elevated when it starts to impact real economic activity, which is why we consider it in the first place.

I think the best measure of real-time economic activity comes from the monthly surveys conducted by S&P Global and the Institute for Supply Management. The surveys of business executives from the manufacturing and service sectors are used to compile an index called the Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI), which gauges the strength in business activity. A level above 50.0 indicates expansion or growth, while one below implies contraction. S&P Global released its Flash survey on Friday, which is a preview of the results at the month end. Shockingly, service sector activity stalled abruptly with the PMI falling to a 25-month low of 49.7. Sentiment, which is being driven by the Trump administration’s policies, is adversely impacting activity.

According to S&P Global, the combination of the two suggests GDP growth of just 0.6%, which is dangerously close to contraction, but this is for just one month’s activity.

Chris Williamson, the Chief Business Economist for S&P Global, put it plainly when he said that “Optimism about the year ahead has slumped from the near-three-year highs seen at the turn of the year to one of the gloomiest since the pandemic. Companies report widespread concerns about the impact of federal government policies ranging from spending cuts to tariffs and geopolitical developments.”

As an investor, I am not going to move from offense to defense based on one high-frequency economic data point, especially when the fuel for the collapse in sentiment has predominantly been rhetoric designed to instigate emotion on both sides of the aisle. That said, if it continues into the coming days and weeks, it will require a more defensive posture beyond positioning for what now looks like another routine pullback in the major market indexes. I find it hard to believe that the Trump administration will continue to pursue policies so draconian that they may undermine a soft landing for the economy, as well as potentially the bull market, because it would also undermine the rest of the agenda.

data.title: Valley National Bancorp: Performance Improvements, But Still Avoiding Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

Valley National Bancorp (NASDAQ:VLY) is a regional bank that has seen its share of volatility since the regional banking crisis nearly two years ago. In addition to its common shares, the bank offers three separate preferred shares. The Series A preferred shares (NASDAQ:VLYPP) are set to float in July. The Series B preferred shares (NASDAQ:VLYPO) are currently floating with a dividend yield of over 8%. The bank issued a fixed rate reset Series C preferred share (NASDAQ:VLYPN) last year that will be reset in 2029. I was cautious about the bank back in October and while the preferred shares are either about to or currently yielding above 8%, I believe there are still too many risks to warrant an investment at this time.

Valley National Bancorp Financial Results

The fourth quarter was generally better for Valley National Bancorp than in previous quarters. With the Fed easing back on short term interest rates, Valley National’s asset yield dropped by 23 basis points to 5.75%, but the borrowing yields dropped by an even greater 37 basis points to 3.85%. The 14-basis point improvement in net interest spread also translated over to net interest margin, which improved 6 basis points to 2.92%. While the bank’s interest spread and margin are still recovering from higher interest rates in 2022 and 2023, the net interest margin is getting close to returning to pre-pandemic levels.

Just as was the case with changes in yield, interest income and expense behaved the same way in the fourth quarter. Valley National saw its interest income fall by $26 million to $835 million, while interest expense fell by a greater $38 million to $412 million. The $12 million increase in net interest income (interest income less interest expense) represented the third consecutive quarter of net interest income growth. Net Interest income remains much higher than where it was before and during the pandemic and is approaching the late 2022 high.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

In 2022, Valley National Bancorp experienced a surge in both loans and deposits due to its acquisition of Bank Leumi. After that, loan growth remained tepid with loan balances declining in the last two quarters of 2024. Loan balances also declined by more than 3% for the full year, which some investors may find concerning, but I was somewhat pleased with due to the direction of the bank’s deposit balances.

Like loans, deposits have been choppy, and the bank has had a tough time holding deposits in a higher rate environment. The bank’s deposits dropped by 0.6% in the fourth quarter but rose by 1.7% for the full year. The increase in deposits while simultaneously decreasing loans is of help to the bank as the loan to deposit ratio has been a concern of mine for some time.

The loan to deposit ratio is a good indicator of how much of the bank’s deposits are pledged towards loans. If the ratio is higher, the bank will need to turn to external financing for funding loan growth. External financing is more expensive than deposits and can hurt earnings. While deposits grew and loans shrank during 2024 to help the ratio, the bank’s loan to deposit ratio remains rather elevated at 96%. The bank’s dependence on external financing did not change much in 2024, but most of the short-term debt was shifted to long term borrowings, signaling that the bank is expecting to be at these elevated loan to deposit ratios for the foreseeable future.

Why Avoid the Preferred Shares?

The improvement in Valley National’s financial results has shown that the risk of the preferred dividends is low, so why not buy the preferred shares? While my position on the preferred shares has moderated, all three issuances of preferred shares trade above par, with the Series B callable now and the Series A callable at the end of June. Even the Series C is a preferred share I don’t like because of the premium, despite not being callable for several years. I don’t see any additional upside to share prices, even if interest rates tumble, so for that reason combined with the risks, I am rating the preferred shares a hold.

Risks to Valley National Bancorp

While Valley National Bancorp has been making progress on managing the concentration of nonowner occupied commercial real estate loans, I remain concerned. Nonowner occupied CRE still accounts for 25% of total loans and the bank’s nonaccrual loans continue to rise with nearly the entire variance coming from this segment. Combined with the fact that the bank’s ACL ratio is below its peers despite recent growth, I have concluded that the bank’s common shares are rated a sell because I believe that loan performance will ultimately eat into earnings.

Conclusion

The last two quarters of financial results have been an overall positive for Valley National Bancorp. The bank has benefited from lower short-term rates with improvements in interest spreads and growth in net interest income. Despite the improvements, the concentration of nonowner occupied commercial real estate loans and their continued performance erosion presents an ongoing threat to earnings. The preferred shares have a good yield, but they are priced too high. Therefore, I remain on the sidelines with respect to Valley National Bancorp.
